evidence_quotes.quote: Some payment processors are actively pressuring companies to remove entirely legal content from their platforms; effectively enforcing a kind of private censorship.
Most products are now sold as subscriptions or licenses, rather than permanent purchases.
There’s also the problem of unilaterally changed Terms of Service (TOS). Users often have no real choice: either accept new terms or lose access to products they've already bought.
We’ve already seen this with digital games, music, and video content disappearing after platforms close or remove access.

paragraphs.text: I want to highlight a growing issue: payment processors appear to be enforcing various forms of censorship across multiple platforms, including Steam, Getchu DL, Denpasoft, Pixiv, Toranoana, DLsite, Fantia, OnlyFans, Fanza, pictSPACE, Melonbooks, Aellune, Niconico, WikiLeaks, Pornhub, Itch, Patreon, and many others. This is deeply unfair. Whats the point of owning money if you cant use it freely, just because credit card companies or processors disapprove of certain content, even when its fully legal? If something is legal, I should be able to buy it, regardless of others personal or moral opinions. No one should be restricted from using their money to legally purchase content, whether its art, media, or games. Some payment processors are actively pressuring companies to remove entirely legal content from their platforms; effectively enforcing a kind of private censorship. This doesnt just impact individual creators; it undermines consumer rights and free expression. Another major issue is how little we truly own today. Most products are now sold as subscriptions or licenses, rather than permanent purchases. From music and games to appliances, everything is becoming temporary. Even basic household devices like washing machines and fridges are now internet-connected, app-dependent, and cloud-reliant. Many no longer function properly, or at all, without Wi-Fi. This raises serious privacy concerns and creates huge usability issues. What happens if the app gets discontinued, or the company shuts down? You could lose access to devices or products you already paid for. We've already seen this with digital games, music, and video content disappearing after platforms close or remove access. Theres also the problem of unilaterally changed Terms of Service (TOS). Users often have no real choice: either accept new terms or lose access to products theyve already bought. This erodes consumer trust and ownership rights. Returning to the issue of censorship, this directly undermines freedom of speech and expression. In democratic societies, these freedoms are essential; they allow people to share ideas, criticize institutions, and engage in meaningful debate. When private companies censor content based on "moral values," even if that content is legal, it restricts creativity, silences dissent, and limits the diversity of voices and stories in our culture. Censorship is not only unfair, it can be discriminatory. For example, removing legal horror-themed content simply because someone finds it disturbing is no different, in principle, from censoring content based on race or identity. Similarly, many censored works include sensitive or marginalized voices, such as content related to the LGBTQ+ community. Silencing these perspectives reinforces inequality and blocks social progress. Not all controversial media promotes harm; often, it reflects it, questions it, or tells stories of resistance and resilience. A violent video game may not glorify violence, but rather offer a powerful commentary on trauma or liberation. Censorship should not be used to enforce corporate morality over legal standards. People are expected to follow the law, not the shifting moral codes of private companies that dictate what we can buy, watch, read, or think. Suppressing art, rewriting history by blocking access to certain books, or censoring voices that challenge the status quo is not how a free, democratic society should operate. Thanks for taking the time to read this.
